title: Malleable Continuum
wikitext:
[]: Malleable Continuum             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:18 pm Evangelion has breached so many continuums that it's hard to keep track of all of them. We have NGE, EoE, Death, the Classified Information... Evangelion now spans so many different areas that it's hard to keep up with all of it. There's so much information that needs to be pieced together that it really makes one wonder if all of it was planned out from the start, or if Anno simply made the universe malleable enough that more information could be inserted into it without breaking the continuum barrier. Did Anno plan out all of the classified information, EoE, and all this other information from the start, or is he still making it up as he goes along?

[]:             Quote Post by Dima » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:24 pm I believe he still making it up as he goes along.I find it really impossible to have thought all about it from the start.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:27 pm Personally, I'm inclined to connect it to Ender's Game. Orson wrote each book within a matter of days, only stopping to eat and sleep. He actually didn't make any notes while working on it; each book was only what he could contain in his head at a time. Perhaps Anno is doing the same thing, working on it only as long as he can contain the continuums in his head, and returning to the ideas when he can't. Still, how do you leave holes to be filled in something without making your audience feel empty on the inside? There was so much information that was added; EoE and the Classified Information, to name a few.

[]:             Quote Post by Dima » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:48 pm Nuclear Lunchbox wrote: Personally, I'm inclined to connect it to Ender's Game. Orson wrote each book within a matter of days, only stopping to eat and sleep. He actually didn't make any notes while working on it; each book was only what he could contain in his head at a time. Perhaps Anno is doing the same thing, working on it only as long as he can contain the continuums in his head, and returning to the ideas when he can't. Still, how do you leave holes to be filled in something without making your audience feel empty on the inside? There was so much information that was added; EoE and the Classified Information, to name a few.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:50 pm NGE and EoE (pretty much the planned ending to the series) seemed to be pretty self-contained stories, while things like the Classified information and the manga and whatever are certainly nice additions, they aren't really key or vital for what Anno wanted to create with Evangelion. I myself haven't read the manga, seen Rebuild, etc. and i don't really feel like i'm missing out on anything. So i'm afraid i don't really understand what you mean.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Apr 19, 2013 3:55 pm Dream wrote: So i'm afraid i don't really understand what you mean.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Apr 19, 2013 5:26 pm Most of those things in the CI are strongly implied in the original, and are merely confirmed by it.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Apr 19, 2013 5:30 pm Mr. Tines wrote: Most of those things in the CI are strongly implied in the original, and are merely confirmed by it.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Apr 19, 2013 6:32 pm Ambiguity and confusion like this is why we have the Tiers of Canonicity. Information and indeed continuities are overruled by information and continuities of higher tiers.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Fri Apr 19, 2013 7:15 pm There was an outline for the whole show, but it mainly revolved around Shinji, Rei, and Yui. Everything else seems tailored to support that basic plot outline and make it happen in a sensible fashion. I know there MUST have been some sort of series of notes about the existing world before they rolled too far into production, because there are times when Anno would only supervise someone else's writing of an episode rather than writing it himself. (Episode 4 comes to mind, which Anno let other writers write for themselves. But to be fair there wasn't too much about NERV or SEELE in that episode either.) Everything else, like Asuka's character arc, some of (but not all of) Kaji's findings, and some of whatever it was Misato was feeling throughout the show feels fleshed out as they came to that episode instead of set in stone prior to writing. It's also important to note that it is possible that not everything in the show was conceived by Hideaki Anno himself. Sure, he was head writer, chief director, and chief editor, but this means he would either allow or reject other people's ideas. (The show does have more people than just Anno listed in "writers," after all.) Anno would ultimately have been responsive for what was included in the show, but (much as he is doing now with the NME series,) others would help him conceive of this stuff with him. This makes for more crazy ideas faster than one man can think up of on his own. This is just part of the creative process, though. I can't claim too much of anything, as I wasn't there. But Anno certainly had help conceiving of the show and worked with other staff writers and story artists in order to finish each episode's script. (As proof, some of these folks are even listed in the credits as part of the writing team.) Now, as to why the story continuity keeps changing from one "platform" to another, I think most of it has to do with the differences between EoE and EoTV, and the writers simply not wanting to tell the exact same story the exact same way twice. Sadamoto's Manga work clearly follows different trains of thought than the anime series, but then, why tell the same story twice? The same would go for the video games and such. People have already seen the anime, so why have the writers concern themselves with being exactly the same as the anime? Why buy a new game or manga with old stories everyone's already heard?

Article: Malleable Continuum
 There was an outline for the whole show, but it mainly revolved around Shinji, Rei, and Yui. Everything else seems tailored to support that basic plot outline and make it happen in a sensible fashion. I know there MUST have been some sort of series of notes about the existing world before they rolled too far into production, because there are times when Anno would only supervise someone else's writing of an episode rather than writing it himself. (Episode 4 comes to mind, which Anno let other writers write for themselves. But to be fair there wasn't too much about NERV or SEELE in that episode either.) Everything else, like Asuka's character arc, some of (but not all of) Kaji's findings, and some of whatever it was Misato was feeling throughout the show feels fleshed out as they came to that episode instead of set in stone prior to writing. It's also important to note that it is possible that not everything in the show was conceived by Hideaki Anno himself. Sure, he was head writer, chief director, and chief editor, but this means he would either allow or reject other people's ideas. (The show does have more people than just Anno listed in "writers," after all.) Anno would ultimately have been responsive for what was included in the show, but (much as he is doing now with the NME series,) others would help him conceive of this stuff with him. This makes for more crazy ideas faster than one man can think up of on his own. This is just part of the creative process, though. I can't claim too much of anything, as I wasn't there. But Anno certainly had help conceiving of the show and worked with other staff writers and story artists in order to finish each episode's script. (As proof, some of these folks are even listed in the credits as part of the writing team.) Now, as to why the story continuity keeps changing from one "platform" to another, I think most of it has to do with the differences between EoE and EoTV, and the writers simply not wanting to tell the exact same story the exact same way twice. Sadamoto's Manga work clearly follows different trains of thought than the anime series, but then, why tell the same story twice? The same would go for the video games and such.
